sources_audited.note: Direct fetch 403s (BLS blocks programmatic access); verified via reader proxy. All cited figures confirmed exactly: total employment 2,530,820; Software and Web Developers/Programmers/Testers 27.35% at $125,010 mean; Computer Support Specialists 7.51% at $64,670; Computer and Mathematical 50.57% at $114,400; Management 14.77% at $180,950; Business and Financial Operations 13.79% at $104,810.

Verified via reader proxy after direct 403. Exact quote confirmed: 19.5 percent growth, ~487,600 jobs, second largest increase of all 292 detailed industries; article also confirms the industry as a main driver of the fastest-growing PST sector and reserves AI-limits-employment language for sales and administrative support, as the packet claims.

169 MSP M&A transactions in 2025 with PE in 69% of disclosed deals confirmed; valuation premiums for recurring revenue, 24+ month contracts, and low customer concentration confirmed; named repeat acquirers (Magna5, Integris, Coretelligent, Ntiva) all present.

73% of privately held companies planning transition within a decade ($14T), ~50% with a detailed plan, 78% lacking a formal transition team, and 2.9M businesses owned by individuals 55+ all confirmed. The memo narrative's separate '41% of small-business owners are 55 or older' figure does NOT appear on this page; it drives no primitive (see caveat).
Controlled experiment confirmed verbatim: 95 developers, Copilot group 55% faster (1h11m vs 2h41m, P=.0017), completion 78% vs 70%.
Eloundou et al. abstract confirmed: ~80% of U.S. workforce with at least 10% of tasks affected, ~19% with at least 50% affected.

Verified in browser (Gartner 403s programmatic fetch). Body reads 'up to 40% of enterprise applications will include integrated task-specific agents by 2026, up from less than 5% today.' The packet's flat '40% by 2026' drops the 'up to' qualifier, and this is a vendor prediction, not a measurement; the packet correctly uses it only as a directional adoption-speed signal for rho and o.

Verified in browser. 13.5% growth to $6.31T in 2026 confirmed; GenAI-driven outsized software gains (GenAI model development spending growth forecast to more than double year-over-year) and IT services at $1.87T confirmed. Worldwide all-category forecast; corroborating context for d5 only, as the packet states.

BCG survey of 300+ IT leaders confirmed: expected budget growth cut from average 4% to 2.4%, more than 2 in 5 deferring discretionary projects, nearly two-thirds ranking cost management a top priority.

Verified in browser (CAPTCHA blocks proxies). Confirmed: ~one quarter of McKinsey global fees from outcome-based pricing amid AI compressing analytical hours; Bain AI/tech-enabled revenue ~30% projected toward 50%; BCG ~20% of 2024 revenue to ~40% by 2026. Note the article's retention-share interpretation is the packet author's inference, not the article's own claim (see caveat).

caveats: The memo's firm-availability narrative states 'roughly 41% of small-business owners nationally are 55 or older,' a figure that does not appear on the cited S5 page (which reports 2.9 million businesses owned by individuals 55+). It is an unsourced narrative statistic; it drives no primitive and s5's base does not rest on it, so it fails the materiality test, but it should be sourced or dropped in any future attempt.

The q bridge equates McKinsey's ~25% outcome-based fee share with 'ceding roughly a quarter of the AI productivity dividend to clients.' An outcome-based fee share is not a measurement of dividend retention, so S11 functions closer to a disclosed analogy than a population-to-population proxy. The direction of error is honestly stated (likely overstates 541512 retention), the low end (0.18) spans the plausible correction, and treating q as ESTIMATE rather than PROXY changes neither the base tier (L is set by H=5.16, and C at base 6.0 stays above the PRIORITY floor) nor the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action, so this is not material.

Gartner's S8 prediction reads 'up to 40% of enterprise applications' and is a vendor forecast rather than a measurement; the packet quotes it as a flat '40% by 2026' and leans on it for both rho and o. The use is disclosed as directional-only, but both D-side and H-side primitives share this single optimistic vendor signal.

s5 and q both draw on the same MSP M&A evidence (S4) and share the recurring-revenue driver; the packet discloses this correlation in risks_and_uncertainty, but the envelope treats their ranges as independent, so the joint downside (both marked down together) is only narratively, not numerically, represented.

S9 is a worldwide, all-category IT spending forecast whose 2026 growth is led by AI data-center hardware; its support for 541512-specific service demand is corroborating context only, as the packet itself acknowledges.
BLS (S1, S2) and Gartner (S8, S9) block direct programmatic fetches; all four were verified via reader proxy or browser and match the cited content.
summary: Mechanics pass cleanly (identity, score, and memo all reproduce). All ten primitive-cited sources were reopened and every one is reachable and supports the specific values, populations, and dates claimed, including exact-figure confirmation of the BLS OEWS occupation mix, the 19.5%/487,600-job BLS projection, the Copilot experiment, the MSP deal tracker, and the MBB outcome-based-pricing shift. Evidence-state discipline is genuinely good: only the dataset anchor l is OBSERVED, every researched primitive is PROXY with a stated bridge and error direction or a disclosed ESTIMATE, e is honestly presented as sourceless judgment, and no unknown is scored as zero. The lens is the stated default with no evident shopping, construct separation is clean (implementation friction confined to rho, pricing/retention to q, demand to d5 and o), ranges are wide and honestly produce a CONDITIONAL-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY interval with no robust tier claimed, and the driver/weakness pair follows directly from the registered evidence. The weakest link is the q bridge, which converts an outcome-based fee-share statistic into a retention share by analogy, and one unsourced narrative statistic (41% of owners 55+) appears in the memo; neither changes the PRIORITY base tier or the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action under the materiality test, so both are caveats. This is exactly the estimate-heavy but honest record the methodology expects: publishable with caveats.
